text: The patient is positioned in a left lateral decubitus position for the elective adrenalectomy. Four laparoscopic ports are visibly placed along the left costal margin.

A laparoscopic liver retractor is used to elevate the right lobe of the liver, providing clear exposure of the large adrenal mass. The surgeon incises the peritoneal reflection up to the diaphragm, including the triangular ligament, to facilitate access to the adrenal gland.
The surgeon dissects the space between the adrenal gland and the inferior vena cava, exposing the right adrenal vein. The right adrenal vein is then carefully isolated from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to carefully divide the isolated right adrenal vein. The medial dissection is extended upwards, with attention to small vessels arising from the inferior vena cava. A second adrenal vein is encountered, isolated, and divided.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the inferior and lateral attachments of the adrenal gland, ensuring the gland is isolated with a healthy rim of periadrenal fatty tissue visible around it.

The surgeon encounters bleeding while dissecting the posterior adrenal gland, using a suction device in the left hand to control the bleeding. Careful dissection is performed to ensure no tumor is left in the retroperitoneum despite the challenging conditions.

The surgeon alternates dissection between the lateral and superior attachments of the adrenal gland, using a suction device to lift the gland while achieving hemostasis with the opposite hand. The adrenal tumor is placed in a retrieval sac and extracted from the abdominal cavity, with confirmed negative tumor margins.
